topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights seemed to mock me, casting a harsh, unforgiving glare on my canvas. My piece, a raw depiction of urban decay, hung center stage, surrounded by other, utterly discordant works. A man with an irritatingly smooth voice was explaining the piece, gesturing with an air of proprietary ownership. I felt a slow, hot pressure building behind my eyes. Every muscle in my jaw tightened until it ached. I wanted to stride over, to snatch the frame, to shatter the polished surface. My knuckles were white where I gripped my portfolio bag. "This is mine," I managed to hiss, the sound thin and shaky, but the tremor in my voice betrayed the storm brewing inside me. The audience, oblivious, offered polite, vacant nods.

[story 2]

I stood at the edge of the room, my breath catching in my throat, watching the crowd mill about the unauthorized exhibition. The sheer audacity of it made my stomach clench. Someone had taken my vision, my painstakingly crafted world, and placed it on display as if it were common property. A deep, visceral churning rose in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I took a step forward, my boots clicking sharply on the marble floor, the sound cutting through the hushed reverence of the onlookers. "You have no right," I stated, my voice low, vibrating with a sudden, sharp intensity. I could feel the heat rising up my neck, a burning sensation that demanded release. I clenched my fists so tightly that my nails dug crescent moons into my palms.

[story 3]

The velvet rope felt like an insult, a flimsy barrier against the invasion. My eyes darted from the painting to the curator, whose smile seemed too wide, too knowing. A fierce, cold anger began to crystallize in my core, solidifying into something sharp and brittle. I walked directly toward the display, ignoring the murmurs of the attendees. When I reached the painting, I didn't touch it; instead, I leaned in close, my shadow falling across the vibrant colors. "Remove it," I commanded, the words clipped and devoid of warmth. A tremor ran through my hands, not of fear, but of sheer, uncontained force. I could feel my pulse hammering against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat against the silence of the gallery, demanding an immediate correction to this profound wrong.
